narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 61.8% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, and background details like your middle-class family income and plans to attend full-time, without considering gender or race. The main reason for this outcome was your relatively low class standing in your third year of undergrad, where you ranked in the second decile—much lower than the typical fifth or sixth decile among students who go on to pass the bar. This stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward predicting failure, as it signals potential challenges in sustaining high performance through law school and the bar. Your first-year decile of third place added to this concern, though less forcefully, since it also trailed the average sixth decile for successful bar takers. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 35.5 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success because it's close to the 37.3 average for those who pass, showing solid reasoning ability. Your undergrad GPA of 3.20 was nearly on par with the 3.24 average for passers, so it didn't sway things much either way. While your middle income aligns with over a third of bar passers and full-time status matches nearly all of them, these didn't outweigh the decile impacts. Compared to accepted applicants, who typically have stronger year-end rankings, your profile suggested higher risk of bar struggles despite your other strengths. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's just the model highlighting patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening later academic showings through grad courses or targeted prep, and reapplying next cycle with an updated profile that addresses these key areas. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by focusing on these steps, and we're here to support your next application.
